qa_pairs.question: What actions are considered abuse of the file age tracking system on the clusters?
qa_pairs.answer: Periodically running a recursive `touch` command on your files to prevent them from being flagged for expiration, and similar tactics aimed at circumventing the purging policy, are considered abuse.

qa_pairs.question: How do staff deal with users who attempt to circumvent the /scratch purging policy?
qa_pairs.answer: Our staff have methods for detecting such tactics. Users who employ these techniques will be asked to move the offending data from /scratch to another location.

qa_pairs.question: Are there methods for detecting attempts to avoid the /scratch purging policy?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, our staff have methods for detecting tactics like periodically running a recursive `touch` command and similar techniques aimed at circumventing the purging policy.

qa_pairs.question: When copying data from /scratch to /project, which commands are usually sufficient?
qa_pairs.answer: In most cases, `cp` or `rsync` will be sufficient to copy data from /scratch to /project.

qa_pairs.question: What issues can arise when using `cp` or `rsync` to copy directories containing symbolic links from /scratch?
qa_pairs.answer: If you have symbolic links in /scratch, copying them with `cp` or `rsync` will cause problems since they will still point to /scratch after being copied.

qa_pairs.question: What is the recommended method for safely copying a directory with symbolic links from /scratch to /project?
qa_pairs.answer: To safely copy a directory with symbolic links, you can use `tar` to make an archive of your files on /scratch and then extract this archive in /project.

qa_pairs.question: Can you provide an example command for safely copying a directory with symbolic links from /scratch to /project using `tar`?
qa_pairs.answer:
Yes, you can use the following commands:
```bash
cd /scratch/.../your_data
mkdir project/.../your_data
tar -cf - ./* | tar -C /project/.../your_data -xf -
```
